---
title: "Applications of Virtual Store Technology at NASA"
---

## Mature implementations

Collection-level aggregated chunk manifests have been successfully implemented at PODAAC for consistently gridded 
Level 3 and 4 datasets.  To date a total of ten PO.DAAC datasets have been virtualized as detailed in a chapter 
"Using Virtual Datasets" in the PO.DAAC online cookbook (https://podaac.github.io/tutorials/quarto_text/UsingVirtualDatasets.html). 


One of the primary advantages of using virtual data stores (VDS), is that allows users to interact with entire datasets 
programmatically and holistically, e.g. in their Jupyter notebooks and Python scripts, whereby one can bypass the task of downloading, 
wrangling and merging data from all the individual files. Take for example one of PO.DAAC’s gridded sea surface temperature
(SST) dataseti called OSTIA from the GHRSST project. 
It contains >15,000 daily files from a 41 year times series and is over 11 TBs. Ideally what is desired is a single line of code, 
to access all the variables in the dataset 
(e.g. SST, SST uncertainty, and masks) in neatly organized multidimensional arrays (in this case 3D for latitude, longitude, and time), 
that then can be subsetted to desired regions and timeranges. In a nutshell, the PO.DAAC VDSs provide seemless access to just the 
desired and specific pieces of a time series, regardless of whether the user in inside or outside the NASA Earthdata Cloud, 
improving access and computation without need to download massive datasets and operate on them in a file-by-file method 
as traditional science workflows often do.

The PO.DAAC VDS implementation model represents a mature approach and is recommended as a baseline implementation for similar data types 
across other DAACs.


[ADD ME: PODAAC Virtual Stores Documentation @DeanHenze @edshred2000]

**Characteristics:**

- Consistent spatial and temporal resolution across each variable
- Uniform chunk grid and compression scheme across each variable
- Chunk manifest generated at collection level, spanning many granules

## Developing implementations

### Displacement data (NISAR)

Synthetic aperture radar displacement products from ASF are an active area of development for virtual store approaches.

[ADD ME: NISAR Virtual Store Documentation @jhkennedy]

### L2 Collections at PODAAC

[ADD ME: PODAAC L2 Virtual Stores Documentation @DeanHenze @edshred2000]

### Architectures for Ongoing Data

Appending new data to an existing virtual store requires different strategies depending on the underlying technology:

- **Icechunk**: supports transactional appends natively
- **Kerchunk**: typically requires overwriting the reference JSON

### Non-uniform data

Some NASA collections present additional complexity:

- Varying compression schemes and chunk grids across a logical array (usually across granules). We often call this "quirky" data.
- L2 (often orbital swath data) with varying coordinates and projections.

These patterns are under active investigation. 

[ADD ME: More about those developments from @maxrjones and @d-v-b]

## Out of scope / not currently supported

*(Specific data types or patterns that are not currently supported will be enumerated here as they are identified.)*
